_meta.explanation: The rule 'The min_snr_5 weighting function assigns higher loss weights to smaller t values (near data), with maximum weight occurring at t = 1/sqrt(6) ≈ 0.408.' does NOT hold here. The model assumes the maximum weight always occurs near t ≈ 0.408 and that any other t will yield a strictly lower weight. However, cosine_sample_median() returns t ≈ 0.292, which lies in the SNR-limited region where snr > 5, making the weight = 5/(snr+1), and due to the specific curvature of the function, this t actually yields a weight (≈0.693) higher than at t=1/sqrt(6) (≈0.678).
_meta.curriculum_note: Rule 'The min_snr_5 weighting function assigns higher loss weights to smaller t values (near data), with maximum weight occurring at t = 1/sqrt(6) ≈ 0.408.' holds in the obvious case but breaks here. Distractor B is the hard negative — the rule naively applied.
